coredata.dc:title: Activation of molecular oxygen by triplet states of S,N-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ibrayev, N.
Seliverstova, E.
Amanzholova, G.
coredata.dc:description:
S,N–doped carbon dots were synthesized and they were considered as activators of molecular oxygen, as evidenced by the observed phosphorescence of singlet oxygen upon excitation of solutions in the absorption band of carbon dots. The process of singlet–triplet annihilation develops when O2(
3
Σ g -
) molecules were added to the solution, the efficiency of which depends on the ratio of concentrations of triplet carbon dots and O2(1Δg) singlet molecules. In the presence of plasmonic nanoparticles of Au the phosphorescence of singlet oxygen was increased.
coredata.dcterms:subject: S,N-doped carbon dots
Phosphorescence
Singlet oxygen
Plasmon effect

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education and Science of the Republic of Kazakhstan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is funded by the Science Committee of the Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Republic of Kazakhstan [Grant No. AP09259913].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the formation of S,N–doped carbon dots, TEM image, size distribution and FTIR spectrum of CDs 1:2.

(a) Absorption, fluorescence and (b) long-lived luminescence spectra of CDs1:2 in degassed solutions with different ethanol concentration. In the inset: decay kinetics of long-lived luminescence registered at 430 and 560 nm for CDs 1:2 solution with 75 vol% of ethanol.

(a) Dependence of the intensity of DF (λreg = 430 nm) at registration at τ1 (treg = 20 μs) and τ2 (treg = 100 μs), as well as the phosphorescence (λreg = 560 nm) on the intensity of the exciting light for a degassed solution of CDs 1:2 with 75 vol% of ethanol. The inset: decay kinetics of DF in a magnetic field and without it; (b) decay kinetics of O2(
1
Δ g
) sensitized phosphorescence (λreg = 1270 nm) in solutions with different concentrations of ethanol at atmospheric pressure. Dots – experimental curves, solid lines – modeled curves according to Eq. (5). The inset shows the phosphorescence spectrum of O2(
1
Δ g
) in CDs solution with 75 vol% of ethanol.

(a) Decay kinetics of O2(
1
Δ g
) sensitized phosphorescence (λreg = 1270 nm) at various O2
(
3
Σ g -
)
concentrations in CDs 1:2 solution. Dots – experimental curves, solid lines – modeled curves according to Eq. (5). (b) Decay kinetics of DF of CDs 1:2 (λreg = 430 nm) at various concentrations of O2
(
3
Σ g -
)
. The inset shows the dependence of the DF CDs lifetime (λreg = 430 nm, red curve) and O2(
1
Δ g
) phosphorescence (λre
g = 1270 nm, blue curve) in the same conditions. In both cases concentration of ethanol in CDs solution is 75 vol%.

(a) Absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs 1:2 in the presence of Au NPs of various concentrations; (b) decay kinetics of O2(
1
Δ g
) sensitized phosphorescence (λreg = 1270 nm) at various concentrations of Au NPs in CDs solution at atmospheric pressure.
Lifetimes of CDs 1:2 in degassed solutions with different ethanol concentration recorded at λreg = 430 and 560 nm.

Influence of the concentration of Au NPs (CAu) on the intensities and lifetimes* of DF and phosphorescence in degassed and oxygenated solution of CDs 1:2 (75 vol% of ethanol) and O2(
1
Δ g
) phosphorescence in oxygenated solutions.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para:
*IDF, τDF and IPh, τPh are the intensities and lifetimes of DF and phosphorescence of CDs in the presence of Au NPs, respectively;
I
DF
0
,
τ
DF
0
and
I
Ph
0
,
τ
Ph
0
are the intensities and lifetimes of DF and phosphorescence of CDs in the absence of Au NPs, respectively; IΔ and
I Δ 0
are the intensities of O2(
1
Δ g
) phosphorescence in the solutions with and without Au NPs, respectively; τ Δ is the O2(
1
Δ g
) phosphorescence lifetime.

head.ce:title: Activation of molecular oxygen by triplet states of S,N-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Molecular Nanophotonics, Buketov Karaganda University, 100024 Karaganda, Kazakhstan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Molecular Nanophotonics, Buketov Karaganda University, 100024 Karaganda, Kazakhstan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Universitetskaya str. 28, 100024 Karaganda, Kazakhstan.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
S,N–doped carbon dots were synthesized and they were considered as activators of molecular oxygen, as evidenced by the observed phosphorescence of singlet oxygen upon excitation of solutions in the absorption band of carbon dots. The process of singlet–triplet annihilation develops when O2(
3
Σ g -
) molecules were added to the solution, the efficiency of which depends on the ratio of concentrations of triplet carbon dots and O2(1Δg) singlet molecules. In the presence of plasmonic nanoparticles of Au the phosphorescence of singlet oxygen was increased.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) refer to quasi-spherical or spherical particles with a diameter of less than 10 nm [1]. They are could be represented as a core/shell structure consisting of carbon core with graphite fragments, and shells from various surface functional groups [2]. The unquestionable advantage of CDs is that, along with fluorescence, they also have an afterglow. The quantum yield and the position of both fluorescence and afterglow bands on the wavelength scale can be controlled by directed synthesis of CDs. This effect can be used to generate singlet oxygen and for biomedical applications [3–5], as well as in optoelectronic and photocatalytic devices [6,7].

CDs are a promising type of singlet oxygen photosensitizers. It was demonstrated by many authors that CDs are effective agents for photodynamic therapy (PDT) [8,9], antibiotic therapy and wound treatment [10,11]. A comparison of CDs synthesized by the top-down and bottom-up methods were made in Ref. [12] in order to reveal the relationship between the chemical structure and the photodynamic effect. It has been shown that the aromatic core, together with oxygen-containing functional groups that are forming at top-down synthesis, provides the most effective agents for PDT.

The effect of doping of CDs with nitrogen atoms on the efficiency of oxygen photosensitization was also studied in [13]. It has been shown that the structure of the dots has a noticeable effect on activation and adsorption of molecular oxygen, which were associated with graphitic and pyrrole nitrogen in CDs. Authors of Ref. [5] studied the efficiency of singlet oxygen generation for N- and N,S-doped carbon-containing dots. Increase in the concentration of surface cyano groups promoted the formation of singlet oxygen (O2(
1
Δ g
)) rather than the accompanying hydroxyl or superoxide radicals. This is consistent with the results obtained in [14], where an increase in the concentration of surface C–N groups due to a decrease in the C–O compounds in CDs promotes the preferential formation of singlet forms of oxygen. In [15] N-doped CDs was synthesized from Taixi anthracite. The resulting N-CDs exhibit singlet oxygen yield φΔ = 0.19. Thus, the degree of doping of CDs with N-containing groups plays an important role in the sensitization of singlet oxygen.

Most CDs have good solubility and physicochemical stability in aqueous solutions. However, it is known [16,17] that molecular oxygen in organic solvents has greater solubility and luminescence lifetime. For example, in [18] hydrophobic CDs were synthesized by bottom-up condensation. The resulting CDs make it possible to generate singlet oxygen in chloroform with a quantum yield of φΔ = 0.31 under the irradiation with blue light. In the work of [19] a polymer nanocomposite based on polyurethane and riboflavin-based polymerized CDs was synthesized. The efficiency of singlet oxygen formation in acetone is equal to 0.28.

Despite the active development of the problem of generation of singlet oxygen in CDs with various compositions, the question of the relationship between the chemical structure and the photodynamic mechanism in CDs still remains open. The problem of co-doping of CDs with sulfur and nitrogen for the production of singlet oxygen is unresolved. Its solution will be important for the targeted synthesis of CDs as highly effective, non-toxic and biocompatible agents for PDT.

For the enhancement of the efficiency of photosensitization and to increase the sensitivity of registration of singlet oxygen phosphorescence the phenomenon of localized plasmon resonance of metal nanoparticles (NPs) can be used [20,21]. The authors of [22] studied the distance dependence of the plasmon effect of Ag NPs on O2(
1
Δ g
) sensitization by Rose bengal triplets. In [23] it was established that interactions between the metal and the photosensitizer in the near zone dominate over scattering effects in the far zone during the production of singlet oxygen. Direct generation of singlet oxygen by metal NPs in the absence of an organic sensitizer was demonstrated in Ref. [24].

For CDs, the effect of plasmon NPs was mainly used to increase the efficiency of their fluorescence [25–27]. Composites based on CDs and plasmonic NPs of metal have been used for the detection of mercury ions [28], anticancer drug [29], in bioimaging [30] and etc.

This work presents the results of a study of the sensitization of singlet oxygen by S,N–doped CDs, as well as the influence of the plasmon effect of Au NPs on this process. The electronic mechanisms of interaction of triplet CDs both with each other and with molecular oxygen are considered.

Water-soluble CDs were obtained by microwave synthesis [31]. The molar ratio of citric acid (Sigma Aldrich) and L-cysteine (Sigma Aldrich) was equal to 1:2 (CDs 1:2). Synthesis procedure is described in Supporting information (SI). Also, CDs based only on citric acid were synthesized according to a similar procedure and were denoted as CDs 1:0.

FTIR spectra of synthesized CDs were registered on an FSM 1201 spectrometer (Infraspec). The XPS spectra were recorded on the Nexsa XPS spectrometer (Thermo Scientific). The registration was carried out with an AlKα source with X-ray spot size of 400 μm.

Both aqueous solutions of CDs and solutions with different ethanol volumes (25, 50 or 75 vol%) were prepared. For all solutions, the concentration of CDs was kept constant, which was controlled by the value of the optical density. The concentration of plasmonic NPs in CDs solutions was equal to 10-14, 10-13 and 10-12 mol/L.

Plasmonic Au NPs in ethanol were synthesized by laser ablation of a gold film (with a thickness of 30 ± 0.3 nm) with the second harmonic of an Nd:YAG laser (LQ215, Solar LS). The synthesis details as morphology and absorption of Au NPs are described in the SI (Fig. S1).

The structure and size of CDs were studied using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F/Cs/GIF, Jeol). An accelerating voltage of 200 kV was used to obtain the images. Data on the sizes of synthesized CDs were also obtained by the dynamic light scattering method with a Nano 90S analyzer (Malvern). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of the studied samples were obtained with Mira 3LMU SEM (Tescan).

The zeta-potential of CDs and metal NPs was estimated with a NanoBrook Zeta Pals analyzer (Brookhaven Instr.) and it was equal to −37.5 ± 1.0 mV for CDs1:2, to −13.34 ± 1.0 mV for CDs1:0 and to −17.2 ± 1.0 mV for Au NPs.

Absorption, fast and long-lived luminescence spectra were recorded with Cary 300 and Eclipse (Agilent) spectrometers, respectively. The registration of the long-lived luminescence signal was carried out after 300 μs from the flash of the Xe lamp.

Fluorescence decay kinetics was recorded in the time-correlated single photon counting mode using a Becker&Hickl system. Excitation of the samples was carried out by a laser with λgen = 375 nm and τ = 120 ps. The fluorescence lifetime was evaluated using the SPCImage software (Becker&Hickl).

The decay kinetics of long-lived luminescence was recorded with an FLS1000 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instr.) with UV,Vis and NIR PMTs (Hamamatsu). The samples were photoexcited at λ exc = 350 nm by a laser system based on an Nd:YAG laser (LQ529, Solar LS), an OPO (LP604, Solar LS) and an second harmonic generator (LG305, Solar LS). For the long-lived luminescence measurements the cell with the CDs solution was degassed to a pressure of P≈133,32∙10-5 Pa (10-5 mm Hg) over the frozen solution. The oxygen content in the cell with a liquid sample was changed by inlet of a certain volume of air into the solution through a glass capillary. The oxygen concentration in a solution can be estimated taking into account its solubility in organic solvents ≈2 mM [17].

The synthesized CDs have a spherical shape with a diameter of 5–15 nm (Fig. 1 ), which is confirmed by TEM microscopy and dynamic light scattering data. Previously, we have shown that along with vibrations of carbon and C–H bonds, oxygen-containing (–COO and C = O) groups are presented in the FTIR spectra of CDs based on citric acid only [31].

In the FTIR spectrum of CDs 1:2, absorption bands of carbon and oxygen-containing bonds appear: the band at ∼ 1600 cm−1 is a consequence of vibrations of C = C bonds included in the carbon skeleton of aromatic rings of CDs, absorption at ∼ 1710 cm−1 is manifested due to stretching vibrations of the C = O bond [31]. The broad band at ∼ 3400 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibrations of O–H groups. Bands associated with CDs doping also appear in the spectrum: absorption at 1400 cm−1 refers to stretching vibrations of the cyano group, and at ∼ 2600 cm−1 is associated with stretching vibrations of the –SH groups (Fig. 1).

In the XPS survey spectrum of CDs1:0, carbon and oxygen lines are observed (SI, Fig. S2). In the spectrum of CDs 1:2, lines of nitrogen and sulfur exhibits in addition to the above indicated lines [32,33]. The analysis of the high-resolution C1s, O1s, N1s and S2p bands of the studied CDs are presented in the SI.

An estimation of the atomic concentration of sp2- and sp3-bonded carbon [34] in the C1s spectrum of CDs 1:0 and CDs 1:2 have shown (Table S1), that that for CDs based on neat citric acid, the content of sp3-bonded carbon is 8.21 %, and in S,N-doped CD it is equal to 13.76 %. The content of sp2-bounded carbon is equal to7.33 and 8.72 %, for CDs 1:0 and CDs 1:2 respectively. From these data it is clear that during the synthesis of CDs from citric acid with the addition of L-cysteine, greater carbonization occurs, which will lead to an increase in the number of sp2-carbon. As demonstrated in [13,35], this may enhance the efficiency of singlet oxygen generation in S,N-CDs. Also, the presence of pyrrole nitrogen in the structure of the dots will probably lead to an increase in the photodynamic activity of CDs [13].

In the electronic absorption spectrum of the synthesized CDs 1:2 a maximum at ∼ 350 nm and a shoulder at 250 nm were recorded (SI, Fig. S3). The long-wavelength absorption band is associated with n → π* transitions in CDs, and the absorption in the region of 200–250 nm is associated with π → π* transitions [31]. For a CDs 1:0, the absorption spectrum exhibits as a descending curve in the UV range without pronounced maxima (SI, Fig. S3).

When citric acid and amino-containing precursors are used for the synthesis, carbon-containing nanoparticles are formed, whose luminescent properties are attributed to carbon cores in composition with organic fluorophores [36]. In the case of using of citric acid and L-cysteine, 5-oxo-3,5-dihydro-2H-thiazolo[3,2-a]pyridine-3,7-dicarboxylic acid (TPDCA) and 5-oxo-3,5-dihydro-2H-thiazolo[3,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (TPCA) are the main structural units and actual sources of S,N-doped CDs fluorescence.

Molecular oxygen has a low solubility and lifetime in water (∼4 μs) [16,17], which makes it difficult direct registration of the phosphorescence of singlet oxygen in aqueous media. In this regard, the sensitization of O2(
1
Δ g
) molecules was studied in aqueous-ethanol solutions of CDs.

The measurements showed (Fig. 2 a) that the addition of ethanol does not noticeably affect on the shape and intensity of the absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs 1:2 or CDs 1:0. CDs with 25 vol% ethanol in solution have the most intense luminescence.

The long-lived luminescence of CDs in water–ethanol mixtures was measured in the cell that was degassed to a pressure of 10-5 mm Hg above the frozen solution. For CDs 1:0 in a 75 % ethanol solution, a low-intensity long-lived luminescence band with a maximum at 430 nm and a shoulder in the region of 550–600 nm was recorded. Its intensity is almost in 50 times less than that of CDs 1:2. In oxygenated solutions, at registration of the decay kinetics of singlet oxygen sensitized phosphorescence at 1270 nm, no reliable signal was obtained. Therefore, further studies were performed for the CDs1:2 samples.

The luminescence spectrum of CDs 1:2 consist of two bands. The maximum of the short-wavelength more intense luminescence band exhibits at 430 nm, and the long-wavelength band – at 560 nm (Fig. 2b). When solutions were oxygenated, long-lived luminescence was extinguished.

It can be seen that the short-wavelength band coincides in position and shape with the fast fluorescence spectrum. For all ratios of water and ethanol, its decay kinetics is biexponential with lifetimes of τ1 and τ2 (Table 1 ). The long-wavelength band is better described by a monoexponential law with τPh and it is the phosphorescence of CDs.

The two-component decay kinetics of luminescence at 430 nm indicates that it is formed by two processes. The initial part of the kinetics with a lifetime of τ1 ≈1/2τPh is due to triplet–triplet annihilation (TTA). The long-term part with a lifetime of τ2 = τPh is associated with thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF). In accordance with the works [37,38], oxygen groups in CDs can to induce a decrease in the value of ΔEST and enhance the spin–orbit interaction in CDs, which contributes to the appearance of TADF.

The scheme of the process of long-lived luminescence of CDs can be represented as follows (Eqs. (1-3)):
(1)
T → S0 + hυPhos, (phosphorescence)
(2)
T + T → [T···T] → S1 + S0 → 2S0 + hυADF, (TTA)
(3)
T → S1 → S0 + hυTADF, (TADF)
where S0, S1, T are singlet and triplet states of CDs correspondingly.
To confirm the nature of the observed long-lived luminescence bands, the dependences of their intensities on the intensity of the exciting light were measured (Fig. 3 a).

The luminescence intensity at λreg = 560 nm increases linearly with an increase in the energy of the exciting light, which is typical for phosphorescence. At λreg = 430 nm in the initial part of decay kinetics at treg = 20 μs the dependence of the annihilation DF (ADF) intensity is quadratic, i.e.
I
DF
∼
I
e x c
2
. It is indicates that the ADF signal is the result of the pairwise annihilation of triplet CDs [39,40]. On the long-term part of the DF decay kinetics at treg = 100 µs, a linear dependence of the luminescence intensity on the excitation intensity is observed, as in the case of phosphorescence.

In addition, the TTA is a magnetically sensitive process [41], that can be estimated from the change in the luminescence intensity in a magnetic field (IB ) and in the absence of a field (I0 ) using the formula (4):
(4)
g
( B )
=
I B
-
I 0
I B
× 100 %
where g(B) is the magnitude of the magnetic effect. For measurement we used a neodymium magnet with B ∼ 1 T, which was placed in a holder with a sample.

As the measurement results showed (inset in Fig. 3a) the luminescence intensity at 430 nm in the presence of a magnetic field decreases. The value of the magnetic effect g(B), estimated from the maximal intensity, is about –33 %. The decrease in the ADF intensity in the magnetic field is typically for liquid solutions and it is consistent with the theoretical model of Atkins and Evans [41]. An external magnetic field reduces the rate of spin relaxation in individual triplet particles in the period between their repeated collisions. On the long-term part of the DF decay kinetics (treg > 70 μs) the noticeable magnetic effect was not observed. In this time range, the main contribution to the kinetic curve is given by TADF, which is not affected by the magnetic field. The intensity of phosphorescence at λreg = 560 nm is not depended on the magnetic field. The data obtained confirm the above-mentioned assumption that the initial part of the DF kinetics is the result of the TTA process.

Since the intensities of phosphorescence and TADF are proportional to the concentration of triplets, they are described by an exponential decay law with the similar lifetimes (τ2 = τPh ≈58 μs). The intensity of the ADF is proportional to the concentration of triplets squared. Therefore, it decays with a time of τ1 equal to the half the phosphorescence lifetime (Table 1).

From the Fig. 2b it is seen that the intensity of both DF and phosphorescence in degassed CDs solutions increases with increasing ethanol content. The ratio of DF intensity to phosphorescence intensity increases with the content of ethanol in the solution and it is equal to 1.9, 3.0 and 5.1 for 25, 50 and 75 vol%, respectively. Such grow in the DF intensity is probably due to the fact that in solutions with a large amount of water, the annihilation efficiency of CDs triplets is lower due to the solvation of particles by water molecules. Solvate shells consisting from water molecules do not promote the approximation of carbon particles to a distances of exchange interaction (1–2 Å). Therefore, the intensity of ADF decreases in predominantly aqueous solutions. The decrease in the intensity of phosphorescence with increasing water in solutions is a well-known fact for CDs [42], because water reduces their rigidization, thereby increasing the probability of non-radiative deactivation of excited states of CDs.

To estimate the values of τrise , modeling of the kinetics of oxygen phosphorescence was carried out in accordance with the methodology given in Ref. [43]. Kinetics of O2(
1
Δ g
) phosphorescence in air-saturated solutions of photosensitizers under pulsed laser excitation is described by a two-exponential equation (5) [43]:
(5)
I
( t )
=
I 0
exp
-
t
τ Δ
- exp
-
t
τ
rise
where I0 is the pre-exponential factor, t is the time of registration, τΔ is the decay time, and τrise is the rise time of decay kinetics of singlet oxygen phosphorescence. The calculation results are shown in Fig. 3b as solid curves. The values of τΔ were taken from experimental data. The simulated curves of decay kinetics of O2(
1
Δ g
) agree well with the experimental data (Fig. 3b). The calculated values of τrise are equal to 0.55, 0.38 and 0.25 μs for CDs samples with 25, 50 and 75 vol% of ethanol, respectively. The obtained values are in good agreement with τrise obtained in Refs. [43] for oxygen-saturated solutions of organic sensitizers of singlet oxygen. Since the best singlet oxygen phosphorescence signals were recorded in CDs solutions with 75 vol% of ethanol, this samples was chosen for further studies.

The study of the effect of oxygen concentration on photoprocesses in a water-alcohol solution of CDs showed in Fig. 4 a. Before the start of measurements, the cell with the CDs solution was degassed to a pressure of P≈133,32∙10-5 Pa (10-5 mm Hg) over the frozen solution. In this case no signal of oxygen phosphorescence at a wavelength of 1270 nm (Fig. 4a, red curve) was registered. The oxygen concentration in the cell was increased by the injection of a known volume of air through the capillary. The intensity of O2(
1
Δ g
) phosphorescence increases with a grow in the oxygen concentration in the cell. The average lifetime of O2(
1
Δ g
) is τ Δ=9.4 ± 0.2 μs and remains constant at all oxygen concentrations.

To estimate the rise time of kinetics of the O2(
1
Δ g
) phosphorescence (τrise ) its curves were modeled according to Eq. (5). The calculation was carried out for curves obtained at different volumes of injected air (V). A well-detected luminescence signal was obtained starting from V = 0.06 mL. The calculation showed that with increasing of a concentration of singlet oxygen in the CDs solution, its τrise decreases from 2 to 0.27 μs at complete saturation of sample with air.

The signal of the DF of CDs (Fig. 4b) was enhanced when oxygen was added. At its concentration equal to 0.04 mL, the intensity of the DF reaches its maximum and then is completely quenched, as is its lifetime. The phosphorescence of CDs is quenched when initial oxygen concentrations were added to the solution, and its dynamics cannot be traced, since water strongly scatters the excitation light.

From the obtained experimental data it follows that singlet oxygen is generated due to the energy transfer from the triplet state (T) of CDs to O2
(
3
Σ g -
)
(Eq. (6)). During the collisions with oxygen molecules the long-lived triplet state T generate the quintet, triplet and singlet states of the collision complex
T · · ·
3
Σ g -
1 , 3 , 5
(Eq. (6)) [44]. For a complex in singlet state, the decay with the formation of the S0 state of CDs and singlet oxygen
1
Δ g
is spin allowed.
(6)
T +
3
Σ g -
→
T · · ·
3
Σ g -
1 , 3 , 5
→
S 0
+
1
Δ g

A possible reason for the spin flip in the oxygen molecule is the increase in internal energy, which is achieved due to the interaction of electrons of sp2–carbon in CDs with electrons in the oxygen molecule [35]. The presence of sp2–carbon in the studied CDs was confirmed by XPS data.

The dependence of the DF intensity on the O2
(
3
Σ g -
)
concentration is the result of singlet–triplet annihilation (reaction (7) between triplet-excited CDs and molecules of singlet oxygen formed after its sensitization by CDs:
(7)
T +
1
Δ g
→
T · · ·
1
Δ g
3
→
→
S 1
+
3
Σ g -
→
S 0
+
3
Σ g -
+ h
υ DF

Molecules of singlet oxygen
1
Δ g
transit to the ground state
(
3
Σ g -
)
with emission of phosphorescence (Eq. (8)):
(8)
1
Δ g
→
3
Σ g -
+ h
υ
Phos

At low oxygen concentrations, the number of triplet CDs is still sufficient for the formation of
T · · ·
3
Σ g -
1 , 3 , 5
pairs, which, decaying with the formation of S1 states of CDs, additionally generate their DF [45]. This results in the enhancement of the DF intensity. With an increase in the oxygen concentration, stronger quenching of CDs triplets occurs. This leads to the fact that the efficiency of the processes of both singlet–triplet (7) and intrinsic (2) TTA of CDs decreases, which leads to a decrease in the luminescence intensity of the CDs. Heteroannihilation between sensitizer triplets and O2(
1
Δ g
) molecules has also been observed in other systems [46,47].

The intensity of the phosphorescence and DF of CDs (IDF ) similarly depends on the concentration of plasmonic NPs (Table 2 ). The duration of phosphorescence is reduced by 40 %, and for DF – by 28 %. The observed increase of intensity with a simultaneous decreasing of the lifetime of long-lived luminescence of CDs in the presence of Au NPs is a consequence of a growth of the radiative decay rate of electron-excited carbon particles [48]. Plasmon effects manifest themselves in spin-resolved radiative transitions. In the case of DF, such an effect can occur at the stage of the S1 → S0 radiative transition. For phosphorescence that is forbidden by spin selection rules, plasmon influence is possible due to the fact that the T1 → S0 radiative transition occurs owing to borrowing of the intensity from the allowed S1 → S0 transition [49].

At atmospheric pressure, the intensity of DF of CDs decreases with increasing in concentration of Au NPs (Table 2). At the same time, the duration of the luminescence was reduced on 51 %. This behavior of the DF indicates that the plasmon effect enhances the energy transfer from triplet CDs particles to molecular oxygen [23,50].

The phosphorescence of oxygen in the presence of gold NPs increased (Fig. 5b, Table 2). In this case, the duration of O2(
1
Δ g
) phosphorescence almost does not changed and was equal to τ Δ = 9.2 ± 0.2 μs. It should be noted that, upon photoexcitation of a neat solution of Au NPs without the addition of CDs, singlet oxygen signal was not observed.

The possible reasons of plasmon-enhanced oxygen phosphorescence may be the following factors. The plasmonic effect can increase the sensitization of O2(
1
Δ g
) molecules by CDs [23,49]. In addition, gold NPs can directly enhance the rate of radiative decay of singlet oxygen as a result of the
1
Δ g
→
3
Σ g -
transition. This becomes possible if we take into account that in the oxygen molecule the spin-forbidden transition
1
Δ g
→
3
Σ g -
can borrow the intensity from the allowed
1
Σ g +
→
1
Δ g
transition [49].
A detailed establishment of the mechanisms of the plasmonic effect of metal NPs on the photophysics of molecular oxygen and CDs will be studied in our future work.

CDs based on citric acid and L-cysteine were synthesized. The sizes of synthesized CDs are varied from 5 to 10 nm. The absorption spectrum of S,N-doped CDs shows a band with a maximum at 360 nm, as well as a shoulder at about 240–250 nm. The fluorescence band of the studied CDs exhibits is in the region of 370–600 nm with a maximum at ∼ 430 nm.

The properties of long-lived luminescence of CDs solutions were studied. It has been established that the decay of triplet states occurs as a result of a radiative phosphorescence transition, reverse intersystem crossing from a triplet to singlet-excited state and triplet–triplet annihilation.

The synthesized CDs are considered as sensitizers of singlet oxygen, as evidenced by the observed phosphorescence of molecular oxygen upon excitation in the absorption band of CDs. The generation of singlet oxygen occurs as a result of energy transfer from triplet CDs to O2
(
3
Σ g -
)
. It is shown that when oxygen molecules were added to the CDs solution, the process of singlet–triplet annihilation develops, the efficiency of which depends on the ratio of the concentrations of triplet CDs and O2(
1
Δ g
) molecules.

In the degassed solutions in presence of plasmonic NPs of Au, the intensities of fast fluorescence, DF and phosphorescence of CDs were increased. The increase in the intensity of DF in the presence of oxygen molecules is less than in a degassed solution. This indicates that the plasmon effect enhances the sensitization of singlet oxygen. The phosphorescence of singlet oxygen is also enhanced by the plasmon resonance of Au NPs.

The results of this work help to better understand the mechanism of singlet oxygen sensitization by CDs and may be useful for the development of carbon photoactivators for photodynamic therapy and bioimaging.

N. Ibrayev: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Project administration, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. E. Seliverstova: Investigation, Data curation, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. G. Amanzholova: Investigation, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is funded by the Science Committee of the Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Republic of Kazakhstan [Grant No. AP09259913].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2023.140947.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
